hook: You already know how to check whether a statement is true for a few examples — but what if you need to be absolutely certain it holds for *every* integer, every graph, or every possible input? A single example can never fully convince a skeptic, and that gap between 'seems true' and 'must be true' is exactly what proof fills. In this unit, you'll build the toolkit that mathematicians use to make airtight arguments — and discover that proving things rigorously is less about inspiration and more about choosing the right strategy.
concept: Every topic in this unit is a different battle-tested strategy for convincing any reader — beyond all doubt — that a mathematical statement is true. Together they form the foundation of rigorous mathematical reasoning, captured by the idea of a *formal proof*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to prove an implication $p \Rightarrow q$ by assuming $p$ is true and logically deriving $q$ step by step, building your core proof-writing intuition.
Master the technique of proving $p \Rightarrow q$ by instead proving its logically equivalent contrapositive $\neg q \Rightarrow \neg p$, which is often far simpler to work with.
Apply the strategy of assuming a statement is *false* and deriving a logical impossibility, allowing you to prove results — like the irrationality of $\sqrt{2}$ — that resist direct approaches.
You will learn to handle statements that cover multiple distinct scenarios by proving each case separately and systematically, ensuring no possibility is overlooked.
You will learn to prove statements about all natural numbers — such as formulas for sums or divisibility facts — by establishing a base case and a domino-style inductive step.
Master strong induction, where the inductive step may use *all* previous cases rather than just the last one, and explore how the Well-Ordering Principle underpins both forms of induction.
